Thank you @PatriciaBota for your PR on feature extraction! Although I have suggested some changes to the code, I did not correct all remarks. Before merging your PR to the main branch, I would ask you to accept or discuss the proposed changes and apply them to the rest of the code. Here are some suggestions to match your code with BioSPPy:

- Follow the import structure of BioSPPy
```python
# Imports
# 3rd party
import package1

# local
from .. import module1
```

- Use the same variable name across different functions/scripts. For instance, in file **eda.py**, `pks` could be replaced by `peaks`, as it appears in the documentation.

- The file **frequency.py** extracts features of several frequency bands (0.05-0.1, 0.1-0.2, ...) that are named `FFT_005_01`, `FFT_01_02`, ...  Would there be a different way of naming those features to make their meaning more clear (e.g., VLF, LF, HF, VHF)?

- Also, there are some variable names with uppercase letters. Could you convert them to lowercase? You can also make use of `_` in your variable names. 

- I also noticed the recurrent use of the numpy's function *nan_to_num*. Is this always necessary?

Thank you for your contributions!

Thanks for taking a stab at this. I'm afraid your changes only work for the default worker group and will break adoption for additional worker groups.

When a `DaskCluster` is created the controller creates the default `WorkerGroup`. But the user can create more `WorkerGroup` resources in Python or via `kubectl`. Those worker groups also need to be adopted by the cluster so that when the cluster is deleted the workers get cleaned up too.

I think instead we need to copy labels from the cluster resource onto the worker group when the worker group is created.

Thanks for picking this up. Can you add a test showing we don't destroy intentional cross-staff beaming? If you parse in a musicxml document with cross-staff beams (voice 1 on both hands) then I think we still want the same voice number on export, not voices n and n+1. No?

I have a feeling you might reply that your module uses voice numbers in a different way than musicxml, and you'd prefer not to delete voices in measures that don't need display voices and prefer not to make your voice numbers distinct since you wish to signal analytical unity. But until we create a way to divorce "display" voices from "analytical" voices in #890, I don't see how. I probably need to close #890 and create a more approachable summary after the discussion on #915, but I just didn't get to it yet.
***
From #915:

> Cross-staff music: PartStaffExporter currently bumps all voice numbers in the subsequent part staff to ensure distinct voice numbers, i.e. to ensure the 99% use case of no cross staff. When we WANT cross-staff, PartStaffExporter needs to get that knowledge from somewhere. Something like, per measure, if two music21 voices share the same .id/.group and .retain = True, if they've not already been given the same .sequence, give them the same .sequence and renumber everything else around it. This is a bit like the implementation in flattenUnnecessaryVoices, above, but different insofar as it would need to be calculated per measure over all voices from all PartStaffs in a joinable group.

Nasty problem. See also https://github.com/w3c/mnx/issues/183.

I noticed a few small things and then one bigger breaking thing caused by running `black` on some files.

_Small things and some nits:_

Since we're updating the `Makefile` I compared it with the python project template `Makefile` and noticed we could add / change a few things:

- `find . -name '__pycache__' -exec rm -rf {} +` at the end of the `clean-pyc` command
- We could split out the `validate-docs` command from there if we wanted to have that separated
- `make notes` is missing "build" from the towncrier line, it should be: `towncrier build --yes --version $(UPCOMING_VERSION)`
- nit cleanup (since both seem to work): `tox elint` -> `tox e lint` in `make lint` command

I also noticed some warnings when running `make notes` related to titles in the docstrings [here](https://github.com/ethereum/eth-abi/blob/master/eth_abi/exceptions.py#L27) and [here](https://github.com/ethereum/eth-abi/blob/master/eth_abi/exceptions.py#L45) that can be silenced by just removing the `-----------` which makes it a title. `black` also seems to be happy with that so this works. Unless we want to keep that formatting in which case adding one more `-` since it is too short reduces the noise from the warning to just an "unexpected title" and it isn't as loud of a warning (not bright red). I'd be good with removing them altogether or extending.

---
_Big thing caused by `black` commit:_

I noticed that `bumpversion` can't find the library version anymore because it annoyingly only looks for single quotes in the line `version='3.0.0'` (in `setup.py`) and we (maybe accidentally?) ran `black` on `setup.py` which changed it to double quotes. We should change it back to single quotes as `version='3.0.0'` and then it does find the version correctly.

We also ran `black` on `docs/conf.py` which changed [this line](https://github.com/ethereum/eth-abi/blob/master/docs/conf.py#L26-L26) which we will need to change back to `setup_version = line.split("\'")[1]` once the above `version` change is made.

Unless there's another way to get around it, but currently those are the changes I made locally that got it all building.

I don't think a warning is necessary if we make JSON the default output, but I'll defer to your judgement on this.

> The JSON output is excellent: it shows the user what Pants's view of a particular file/target actually is. That transparency is great for debugging. We might want to make JSON the default?
> 
> I'd recommend two tweaks:
> 
>     1. Rather than `alias`, call it `target_type`, or something similar. "Alias" is an implementation detail.
> 
>     2. I think we want to render default values? That way, we show a complete view of what Pants is doing. It also allows for safer JSON queries, as you're guaranteed the key will exist. If it gets too chatty, we could consider an option like `--ignore-defaults`.
> 

I agree with these suggestions.

- [ ] Make JSON the default output style
- [x] Rename `alias` in output to `target_type`
- [ ] Show default values by default
- [ ] Add `--exclude-defaults` flag (optional)

> In #4861, we mentioned adding a table format in a followup. I'm not sure that would really be necessary - the mix of Raw and JSON is very helpful already.

Agreed. It's human-readable enough, and machine readable with things like `jq`

> In #4861, I pondered
> 
> > Should [we] evaluate the sources and dependencies fields, or show the raw values?
> 
> After seeing this PR, I think no, we should not. `./pants dependencies` already exists for deps, and `./pants filedeps` for sources. It's redundant imo, and also non-trivial, to include those here, including a performance hit.

Agreed, and if you really want that, `jq` + `xargs` are your friends:

```
./pants peek ... | jq -r ... | xargs ./pants filedeps
```

Some large-scale refactoring things:

   * The `Jax` nplike is not much different from the `Cupy` one, and the `Cupy` one is in the `ak.nplike` module. I think the `Jax` one could go here (and it needs to be possible to declare the class without importing `jax`. Whenever you have something that depends on two attributes, like "is an nplike" and "is for JAX", there's always a question of whether to organize them such that all the nplikes are together or to organize them such that all the JAX-related stuff is together. Let's organize it with all nplikes together, because that will enforce that they behave the same way with respect to when the third-party library gets imported. Some nplikes, like the TypeTracer, are in other places, but that's because the TypeTracer is very different from the nplikes for third-party libraries.
   * Modules in `ak._v2.operations.*.*` should each provide one high-level function. See, for instance, `ak_from_parquet` and `ak_metadata_from_parquet`. There's a lot of overlap between these two, but they're in separate files to maintain that structure. So please split `ak_from_lib` back into `ak_from_cupy` and `ak_from_jax` (same for the "to" direction).
   * Sharing code in the _implementations_ of `from_X` and `to_X` is a good idea, and maybe it would be possible to extend this shared code to NumPy as well as CuPy and JAX. The shared implementation sounds important enough to go into `ak._v2._util` as functions named something like "`from_arraylib`" and "`to_arraylib`". Passing in the `module` object is a good idea, too: it could be done in both, the "from" as well as the "to". If it ever becomes necessary to handle special cases for differences between NumPy, CuPy, and JAX, we can check the `module.__name__` to know which one we have (without accidentally triggering an import of one of the others).

Unfortunately, I don't think this is the right approach.  charm-helpers is a library, and this is changing the nature of the library function which currently raises an Exception if there is no answer, and instead will now return None.  All the charms that use this function expect (or rely on) an exception being generated if the function fails, rather than returning None.  This could make charms fail in interesting ways as they would now get a value (None) and try to do something with it, rather than the exception either being handled or stopping execution.

It would be better to handle the error in the calling function to wrap `get_hostname()` in an exception handler rather than changing the API of the library.

> too many individual ones to comment on, but __exit__ and __aexit__ returns [bool, not None.](https://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html?highlight=__exit__#object.__exit__) (or, well, it returns either True or anything else).

FWIW, in typeshed these are typed as `Optional[bool]`. This is really context-dependent: if one of these is typed as returning `None` or `Literal[False]`, then mypy at least can infer some special stuff about the context managers as it knows they won't swallow errors. (refs: they reverted https://github.com/python/mypy/issues/7577 but reachability is changed by this: https://github.com/python/mypy/issues/7666)

alright so I think the sequence here was:
* when `Nothing` was introduced, you had to have `Nothing` typed inputs if they were hooked up to `Nothing` typed outputs, so the link in the previous comment was all that was needed.
*https://github.com/dagster-io/dagster/pull/9112 - relaxed the constraints around `Nothing` deps and allowed Any type inputs to get hooked up to them.

It should be possible for the core to opt-out of the load input when the upstream type is `Nothing`, so i think thats probably the way to confront the problems highlighted in the summary. 

As for this overall change, there was thinking recorded in https://github.com/dagster-io/dagster/pull/9094 around `handle_output` being fired on `None` being potentially desirable. This change would "break" that use case if it exists so maybe that enough of a blocker to dismiss this

@sushimon good work. You're running into a current limitation of the "control boundaries" because right now the "key" is the class name (`'Raise'`) but we need a way to distinguish between exception types for except blocks. However, your current approach breaks the natural recursive structure of the code (and modifies `_control_boundaries` a lot more than necessary).

A cleaner way of resolving this problem is to generalize the "control boundaries" so that they store both the '`Raise'` and the exception class name, and then add all exception handlers to `_control_boundaries` at the start, before recursing into the body statements.

So something like 

```python
try:
    ...
except ValueError:
    ...
except IndexError:
    ...
```

would cause `control_boundaries` to look like

```python
[('Raise ValueError', ...), ('Raise IndexError', ...)]
```

that does make `_visit_jump` more complicated, but I think overall it'll simplify the code.

Here's my summary of the problem and solution -- just wanted to clarify my understanding from the PR description:
1. Paused trials come from scheduler decisions. Once a scheduler decides a trial should be paused, it should basically be ignored by the Tune controller, until the scheduler decides that it's this trial's turn to run again.
2. Previously, it was possible to go directly from paused -> staged -> pending (set by `_schedule_trial_actor` -> running.
    - With this fix, it's now only possible to go from pending -> staged -> running.
    - So, the only route for a paused trial to resume would be: paused -> pending -> staged -> running.

Due to point 2, [should this line be an](https://github.com/ray-project/ray/blob/860a8c9c7b70daaea75e0172a4ccdd23f81146ad/python/ray/tune/execution/tune_controller.py#LL545C12-L545C12) `assert trial.status == PENDING` instead of setting it again? Sanity check that we only ever schedule an actor for a pending trial.

BUT, I think that a `constraints.txt` file is different from a `requirements.txt` in one fundamental way: the entries in a constraints file (or any other lockfile) may never be used independently: they are always used together as a single atomic unit (because you don't know until you run the resolve whether an entry in the lockfile is relevant to a particular requirement in the requirements file: it might be transitive).

This might mean that we don't need a macro, because we don't need to create multiple constraints targets per file?

One high-level thing I'd suggest is that we could actually create a new function in summary_v2.py that's called `histogram_v3()`, and then we can stage these changes in a way that affects that function only, which would make it easier to get them landed in smaller PRs without worrying about it being a breaking change yet for existing `tf.summary.histogram()` users (since there are some other places in TensorBoard that we probably should update before releasing the format change itself, as the design doc describes).

My suggestion would be to start out with a separate change in which we create `histogram_v3()` as basically a copy of `histogram()` but without all the special handling for name scopes and TPU outside compilation.  Essentially `histogram_v3` would look like rolling back `histogram` to the simple version we had before all the TPU issues: https://cs.opensource.google/tensorflow/tensorboard/+/master:tensorboard/plugins/histogram/summary_v2.py;l=69-79;drc=fdeeb395deaa953d2e5e6375c418a84feb05800b

I'd then re-apply this one later change to use LazyTensorCreator, since that's useful even aside from the TPU issues: https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/pull/2899

I'd also create a copy of the bucketing helper function, `_buckets_v3()` which would be used by `histogram_v3()`.

Once we have that set up, we can easily land changes like this that affect only `_buckets_v3()` and have a separate set of tests, etc.  Once it's working and we've vetted it on TPU, we can cut over the public API symbol from the v2 to the v3 function.  How does that sound?

This looks great! The CSS implementation degrades gracefully on IE11 and otherwise the behaviors are quite similar, so I would support migrating to it entirely rather than maintaining two parallel implementations.

Shorter-term the main question is cost/risk of migration – if either are high, we might want to use both at least until 0.15. Longer-term once things are consolidated, we could even move it to the design system as `KTextTruncator`.

> Multiple lines - a long word with no breaks

The behavior for the CSS implementation looks good here. Flagging that this case has been a source of many bugs over the years, and warrants a careful eye:

* https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/issues/5468
* https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/issues/5464
* https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/issues/4419


> There's an issue in IE11 with three ellipsis dots not showing in some situations. It seems to be a font issue.

Would you mind filing a separate issue for this? For some context, here is an overview of the intended behavior:

https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/blob/6903afec0ca3ebdf65fbf0eb96ef5348ed9fc2d2/packages/kolibri-tools/lib/i18n/fonts.py#L589-L603

and the client-side font loading occurs here:

https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/blob/6903afec0ca3ebdf65fbf0eb96ef5348ed9fc2d2/kolibri/core/assets/src/utils/setupAndLoadFonts.js

> Regarding a11y ... for most techniques, it says that the whole text would be read. 

I've always assumed that the best behavior would be to only read the visible text: if we've made a design decision to truncate it visually, that should probably apply to audible text too? That said, I don't think that either choice is "wrong" and both will result in a reasonable experience for all users.

Thanks for creating this pr! This seems like a good feature to have, and you are on the right track to implement it. Before we get into the code itself, let's take a closer look at the expected behaviour. Let's take this example code:
```
schedule.every(10).seconds.until("23:30").do(job)
while True:
    scheduler.run_pending()
    time.sleep(24)
```
Let's say this code is executed at 23:40. What will happen? I guess in the current implementation the job runs once and is canceled afterwards. Is this expected behaviour?

Another scenario: Let's say above code block starts at 23:29:00. The first execution is at 23:29:24 (scheduled execution was 23:29:10), the second execution is at 23:29:48 (scheduled execution was 23:29:34) and the third execution happens at 23:30:12 (scheduled execution was 23:29:58). At all three the times the job fires. Is this expected behaviour?

Answers to these questions could very well be answered as part of the documentation.

The idea of having `config/crypto.h` and define the size of the contexts for different crypto operations sounds reasonable to me, but the downside is that we need to manage the numbers manually (instead of having something like `sizeof(mbedtls_aes_context)` where compiler determines the proper size).

One idea/suggestion on this: 
- We can consider adding a new config `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_CRYPTO_LIBRARY` to indicate to OT core which crypto library is being used, 
   - e.g., we can have `CRYPTO_LIB_MBEDTLS_Vxxx` or `CRYPTO_LIB_PSA` or `CRYPTO_LIB_OTHER` (for vendor/platform specific ones) etc). 
- Then based this config we can include the proper headers and then use `sizeof(mbedtls_aes_context)` for the context constants. 
- Or maybe even directly use `mbedtls_aes_context` as the definition of context depending on the `OPENTHREAD_CONFIG_CRYPTO_LIBRARY` in the core implementation (e.g., in `Crypto::AesEcb` class).

@jameslamb @david-cortes @jmoralez  I like the new "type" argument. However, if I correctly understood the intended changes, they are in gross contrast what usual prediction functions involving link functions do. Sorry for this late input. I think it is important to consider it anyway.

According to `predict.glm()`, type "response" returns values on the scale of the response.  A Poisson regression with log link would return positive values, a binary logistic regression would return probabilities.

Type "link" means, on the other hand, that predictions are made in the link space. For a logistic regression, it would be the logit of the probabilities, for a Poisson regression the log expected counts etc. 

**LightGBM seems to use "link" in the exact opposite, i.e., in the sense that the inverse link is being applied. But this is what is usually be called "response"...**

My suggestions: 

- The default type is "response". Binary logistic will return probabilities. 
- There is either "raw" or "link" but not both. For historic reasons, I tend to "raw", but "link" is fine as well.
- There should be a type = "class" in order to get the majority class for those objectives where it makes sense. `glm.predict()`does not have this, but for instance "predict.glmnet()".
- Type "leaf" and "contrib" are okay.

Side note: `predict.glm()` uses `type = "link"` as the default, i.e., it predicts log odds instead of probabilities for a logistic regression. We should not do it similarly because then every logistic/Poisson/Gamma/Tweedie model would be affected from the change.

Looks good to me and fixes an important issue

Apart from the ones I fixed, there are more operators that use the `.view(-1, ...)` idiom. 

- https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/4c073b09521604e410bac8a0b0fe0f2680724e0d/torchvision/prototype/transforms/functional/_geometry.py#L227
- https://github.com/pytorch/vision/blob/4c073b09521604e410bac8a0b0fe0f2680724e0d/torchvision/prototype/transforms/functional/_geometry.py#L448

I've xfailed some tests for them. Plus, there are more bounding box ops that do this. But we don't need to worry about them, because `.view(-1, ...)` only fails if there is a `0` in the `...` part. For bounding boxes we only do 

```py
>>> t = torch.empty(0, 4)
>>> t.view(-1, 4)
tensor([], size=(0, 4))
```

The ambiguity only arises for segmentation masks, which use the channel dimension as number of objects and that can be `0`.

```py
>>> t = torch.empty(4, 0, 16, 16)
>>> t.view(-1, 0, 16, 16)
RuntimeError: cannot reshape tensor of 0 elements into shape [-1, 0, 16, 16] because the unspecified dimension size -1 can be any value and is ambiguous
```

That might be a good reason to have something in the kernel other than just calling the image kernel. But we can discuss this later.

info on looponfail:

In a configuration file for pytest, the `looponfailroots` option specifies the directories that pytest should monitor for changes when running in the "looponfail" mode. 

The `looponfail` mode is a useful feature of pytest that automatically re-runs tests when changes are detected in the specified directories. It is especially handy during development, as it allows you to focus on writing code and tests while pytest continuously runs relevant tests in the background.

In the given example, the `looponfailroots` option specifies two directories: `tests/` and `trailblazer/`. These directories are monitored by pytest, and if any changes occur within these directories or their subdirectories, pytest will re-run the failed tests. This can help in quickly identifying and fixing issues as you modify your code or tests.

Note that the `looponfailroots` option is optional, and you can specify multiple directories by listing them on separate lines, as shown in the example.

Thank you very much for the update. I played around with the new neptune logger and experiment tracking site and was impressed. It's very useful and there are nice additions since last time I checked.

I have a few code comments below, please take a look. On top of those, I have some more general comments:

### Versions

I saw that you have some code that works with `neptune-client>=1.0`, which I didn't find released on PyPI. So I assume it's for forward compatibility for a yet to be released version. Big :+1:.

That said, I ran into some issues with certain Neptune versions (see one of my code comments). So I think that there could still be an issue with supporting all versions `>=0.9.0`. I would thus like to ask you to check again if it works with all intended versions. Maybe some older ones can be dropped? At the end of the day, you know best which versions should still be supported and how.

### Pickling

A minor inconvenience I found is that the `NeptuneLogger` apparently can't be pickled:

> AttributeError: Can't pickle local object `'NeptuneAuthenticator.__init__.<locals>.session_factory'`

Therefore, a user would not be able to save the whole skorch net on Neptune. If there is an easy way to fix this by doing something with `NeptuneLogger.get_state`, that would be nice, but I'm not sure. On the neptune-client side, these lines seem to be the offender:

https://github.com/neptune-ai/neptune-client/blob/81fcf25b204331ae85b67e19afc78be69ddf3f84/src/neptune/oauth.py#L98-L126

Since a reference to a local function is stored, pickle fails. So if you ever want to fix this, you would have to move this definition to the module level.

### Tests

While running the tests, I got this message multiple times:

> Error in atexit._run_exitfuncs:

Not sure what it means and if it can cause any trouble.

@felix-hilden while I don't know this visitor code well either, if you take a look at `src/black/nodes.py` for the `Visitor` class (which `LineGenerator` subclasses), the `visit` method is the one that dispatches to specialized visitor methods based off the node's type. The `test` node is defined in the grammar here:
https://github.com/psf/black/blob/14072be245b909d2340e13c571e76bf36080cc5a/src/blib2to3/Grammar.txt#L135

So when a node of type `test` is visited, `Visitor.visit` will dispatch to `LineGenerator.visit_test`. For more information, you're better off asking Jelle :)

Even if you do punt on handling ambiguity, the code maintainability might benefit from more closely following the structure of `pants/backend/python/dependency_inference`:

- two rules to create `GoFirstPartyImportPathMapping` and `GoThirdPartyImportPathMapping`
- one rule to go from a single `GoImportPath` to `GoImportPathOwner`, which has an `owner: Address` and (eventually) `ambiguous: tuple[Address, ...]`.
- this rule you have now to use the dependency inference plugin hook

This delineation has been really helpful in Python land for having more focused tests.
